description: The Roman Empire dominated the Mediterranean world during Christianity's formative centuries. Its roads, legal system, and common languages (Greek/Latin) facilitated the spread of the gospel. Persecution was sporadic until Decius (249) and systematic under Diocletian (303-311), ending with Constantine's legalization (313).

The continuation of the Roman Empire in the East after the division of 395. Became the primary patron and protector of Orthodox Christianity, hosting ecumenical councils and defining Christian doctrine through imperial authority.

The last pre-Islamic Persian empire, ruling Mesopotamia and Iran. Christianity (Church of the East) existed as a tolerated minority, sometimes persecuted, sometimes protected. The Sassanids were Zoroastrian and often viewed Christians with suspicion as Roman sympathizers.
The Arsacid dynasty ruling Iran and Mesopotamia before the Sassanids. Christianity entered Parthian territory through Jewish-Christian communities in Mesopotamia, especially Edessa and Nisibis.
East African kingdom that adopted Christianity c.340 under King Ezana through the influence of Frumentius, consecrated by Athanasius of Alexandria. One of the earliest Christian states.

First kingdom to adopt Christianity as state religion (c.301) under King Tiridates III through Gregory the Illuminator. The Armenian Apostolic Church traces its origins to the apostles Thaddeus and Bartholomew.
Caucasian kingdom that converted to Christianity c.330 through Saint Nino. Became part of the Georgian Orthodox tradition; strategically located between Roman and Persian spheres.
Arab kingdom centered on Petra, annexed by Rome in 106 AD. Early Christian communities may have existed among Nabataean populations, especially after Paul's sojourn in Arabia (Gal 1:17).

Brief breakaway state under Zenobia that controlled the eastern Roman provinces. Bishop Paul of Samosata was condemned for adoptionism during this period; Palmyra's fall restored Roman ecclesiastical order.
South Indian dynasty in Kerala where the Thomas Christian community traces its origins to the Apostle Thomas. The Chera rulers were generally tolerant of Christianity alongside Hinduism and Buddhism.
